title: Clearing a blocked bath or basin waste trap
subtitle: Clear a slow-draining or fully blocked waste trap by plunging or removing and cleaning the trap
excerpt: How to clear a blocked bath or basin waste, starting with a cup plunger, then removing and cleaning the waste trap below the basin, and flushing the waste pipe to restore full drainage.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Plumbing for Beginners (1918, Traps, Wastes and Overflows). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A compact waste trap shaped like an upturned bottle that holds the water seal below a basin. The lower section of the body unscrews to allow access for clearing blockages. The water seal in the trap prevents sewer gases from entering the room. Bottle traps are compact but more prone to blockage than P-traps.

The hole near the top of a bath or basin that prevents water spilling over the rim. It connects to the waste pipe below the plug hole and must be blocked during plunging, otherwise the plunger force is lost through the overflow slot rather than dislodging the blockage.

content.text: Most basin and bath blockages are hair and soap in the
below the plug hole, not further down the waste pipe. Start with a cup plunger before dismantling anything. Block the
with a damp cloth before plunging: without this, the plunger pressure vents through the overflow instead of pushing the blockage.

A cup plunger and a damp cloth for blocking the overflow. If the trap must be removed: a bucket and old towels for the water in the trap (a full trap holds about 500 ml). A pair of rubber gloves. If the blockage is further down the pipe: a 5 m drain snake for reaching beyond the trap into the waste run.

Fill the basin with enough water to cover the plunger cup. Block the overflow hole firmly with the damp cloth held by hand. Place the cup plunger squarely over the plug hole and push down firmly, then pull up sharply. Repeat 10 to 15 times without releasing the seal. The surge of water should dislodge a hair or soap blockage in the trap. Run the hot tap and check whether water now drains freely.
Step 2, remove and clean the trap

If plunging does not clear the blockage, put the bucket under the trap. On a bottle trap: unscrew the base of the bottle section by hand (anticlockwise looking up). On a P-trap: undo the large plastic slip nuts at each end of the curved section. Remove the trap and empty the water into the bucket. The blockage is usually a matted ball of hair and soap inside the trap: pull it out and discard it. Rinse the trap under the tap to clear any residue.
Step 3, check the waste pipe beyond the trap

With the trap removed, shine a torch into the waste pipe opening in the wall. If there is a blockage further along, push the drain snake into the pipe. Feed the cable in, rotating the handle clockwise, until it meets resistance. Work the snake back and forth to break up the blockage and pull it back into the basin for removal. Flush with a full bucket of hot water before refitting the trap.
Step 4, refit the trap and test

Refit the trap and hand-tighten the slip nuts. Do not use tools on plastic slip nuts: they are designed to be hand-tight and overtightening cracks the trap body. Run the hot tap for two minutes at full flow to flush the waste pipe. Check under the basin for drips at the trap joints. If a joint drips, tighten the slip nut a quarter-turn by hand.

Bath waste traps are often a combined trap-and-waste assembly hidden under the bath panel. Access requires removing the side panel. The trap and its cleaning procedure are the same as for a basin, but space is tighter. Use the water-pump pliers if the slip nuts have been tightened by a plumber rather than left hand-tight. A bath trap holds more water than a basin trap: have a larger bucket ready.

Remove and clean the basin trap every six months in a household where hair blockages are a recurring problem. A mesh drain cover over the plug hole catches hair before it reaches the trap and reduces the frequency of blockages. Do not pour chemical drain cleaner into a plastic trap: many drain cleaners contain sodium hydroxide, which can soften or crack plastic trap bodies over time.

attrs.symptom: Drain still slow after cleaning the trap and snaking the pipe
attrs.cause: The blockage is further down the shared waste stack or in the external drain gulley.
attrs.fix: Check the external drain gulley outside the house. If it is gurgling or backing up, the blockage is in the drain run between the gulley and the sewer. This requires a longer drain rod set or a drainage contractor.

attrs.symptom: Trap drips at the slip nut joint after refitting
attrs.cause: The rubber washer inside the slip nut has moved out of its recess during reassembly.
attrs.fix: Undo the slip nut, check the washer is seated flat in the recess, and hand-tighten the nut again. If the washer is deformed, replace it; slip-nut washers are available from plumbing merchants.
